Christologically, the universal scope of the indictment — both the northern and southern kingdoms, representing all of Israel — is the background for Paul's universal conclusion: "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Rom 3:23), requiring the universal redemption available "through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus" (Rom 3:24).
23.37: Adultery and blood on hands — murder of children. The twin crimes: covenant infidelity and the sacrifice of covenant children. Christologically, Christ addresses both: his blood cleanses from all sin (1 John 1:7) including the sin of bloodguilt; and his atoning death provides the sacrifice that ends the need for child sacrifice — "he gave himself up as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God" (Eph 5:2).
23.38: Defiling the sanctuary on the same day as the child sacrifice. The liturgical abomination: blood-stained hands in the sanctuary. This is the situation that the author of Hebrews addresses when describing Christ's priestly ministry: he entered not with "the blood of goats and calves but with his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption" (Heb 9:12) — the sanctuary purified not by further sacrifices but by the one sufficient sacrifice.
23.39: Children killed then entering the sanctuary — the same day. The juxtaposition of violence and worship. Christologically, Christ unifies what Israel split: in him, the sacrifice is the entrance into the sanctuary, not a desecration of it. Hebrews 10:19-22: "we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus... as the great priest over the house of God."
23.40: Sending for men from far away — diplomatic messaging and preparation for the visiting lover-kings. The elaborate international courtship of foreign powers as covenant betrayal. Christologically, the movement is reversed: it is God who sends across the cosmic distance — "in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son" (Heb 1:2), "God sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh" (Rom 8:3). The sending that Israel did toward foreign powers, the Father does toward the people.
23.41: The couch, the table spread, the incense and oil — YHWH's gifts used as preparation for foreign diplomatic encounters. The sacred gifts made into secular-diplomatic props. Christologically, the re-consecration of these gifts is the Eucharist: the bread and cup (table spread), the fragrant offering (incense) — all restored to their proper covenantal context in Christ's new-covenant meal.
23.42: The carefree crowd with bracelets and beautiful crowns. The festivity of the pagan diplomatic encounter. Christologically, festivity itself is not condemned but re-consecrated: the wedding feast of the Lamb (Rev 19:9: "blessed are those who are invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb") is the ultimate feast, where the celebration is of the covenant restored rather than the covenant betrayed.
23.43: The worn-out woman continuing to prostitute herself. The exhaustion of covenant infidelity — the cycle that never satisfies and never ends by itself. Christologically, the Samaritan woman at the well (John 4:7-26) is the Oholah/Oholibah figure in the Gospels: five husbands and a current non-husband, the thirst that Jacob's well cannot quench. Christ offers water that ends the cycle permanently.
23.44: Going to the loose woman. The community pattern — everyone going to the prostitute as to a common woman. The normalization of covenant infidelity. Christologically, this normalization of sin is what Paul says Christ came to break: "you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God" (1 Cor 6:11) — the "formerly" of the community that was like Oholah and Oholibah.
23.45: Righteous men judging with the judgment for adulteresses and for women who shed blood. The legal-community judgment. Christologically, the community judgment of the covenant-breaking city anticipates the final judgment before the Son of Man: "when the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the angels with him, then he will sit on his glorious throne. Before him will be gathered all the nations" (Matt 25:31-32). The prophetic judgment of Ezekiel 23 is the temporal expression of the eschatological judgment.
23.46: The assembly brought up against them — terror and plunder. The divine command for the punishing assembly. Christologically, the gathered assembly of judgment in Ezekiel 23 is inverted in the NT gathering: "For where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them" (Matt 18:20). The fearful assembly of judgment becomes the joyful assembly of the new covenant community gathered around Christ.
23.47: The stoning and sword execution of both sisters. The double death sentence of both kingdoms. Christologically, death is the sentence that Christ absorbs: "he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree" (1 Pet 2:24). The double execution of both covenant kingdoms is the double condemnation that one innocent person absorbs on the cross, freeing those who trust him from their own condemnation.
23.48: Removing lewdness from the land so that all women might be instructed not to do as they had done. The instructive purpose of the judgment — the lesson for the nations. Christologically, the removal of lewdness from the land is the negative form of what the sanctifying work of the Spirit accomplishes: "it is God who works in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure" (Phil 2:13). The instruction that judgment provides by fear, the Spirit provides by transformation.
23.49: Bearing the penalty of sinful idolatry — and knowing YHWH: "They shall bear the penalty of your sinful idolatry, and you shall know that I am the Lord YHWH." The closing recognition formula after the double sentence of both sisters. The bearing of penalty is what makes the knowing-YHWH possible. Christologically, Christ bears the penalty (Isa 53:4-5: "he has borne our griefs... he was wounded for our transgressions") so that the knowing of YHWH might be restored: "this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God" (John 17:3). The penalty-bearing and the knowing-YHWH are causally linked through the cross.

24.1: The precise date of the siege: The exact date marking the beginning of Nebuchadnezzar's siege of Jerusalem. Christologically, this precision of divine timing is the background for the "fullness of time" principle: "when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son" (Gal 4:4). YHWH knows the exact moment that his redemptive acts occur — the precision of Ezekiel 24:1 (9th year, 10th month, 10th day) points to the divine mastery of history that culminates in the cross "at the right time" (Rom 5:6: "while we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly").
24.2: Write down the name of this day — the command to record the moment. The accountability of the prophetic word to historical verification. Christologically, the historical specificity of the incarnation is integral to the gospel: "under Pontius Pilate" in the creed, the exact date-markers of Luke 3:1-2 (the 15th year of Tiberius, etc.). The gospel is not myth but dated historical event, just as Ezekiel's oracle is dated historical prophecy.
24.3: The allegory of the rusty pot for the rebellious house. The sign-act mode of prophetic communication — enacted metaphor rather than verbal description alone. Christologically, Jesus consistently uses this same enacted-metaphor mode: cleansing the temple (John 2:14-17), cursing the fig tree (Mark 11:12-21), washing the disciples' feet (John 13:1-17). The prophetic tradition of enacted signs culminates in the incarnation itself — the ultimate sign-act of God acting within history.
24.4: Gathering the pieces into the pot — the choice pieces, thigh and shoulder. The best portions of the covenant community gathered into the vessel of judgment. Christologically, the gathering into the pot of judgment is the gathering into Christ in reverse: where the judgment-pot gathers the people to destruction, Christ gathers the scattered people to salvation (John 11:52: "to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad").
24.5: Pile on the wood, kindle the fire, boil the bones within it. The thorough cooking — nothing spared. The fire under the pot is YHWH's judgment. Christologically, "our God is a consuming fire" (Heb 12:29, citing Deut 4:24) — the fire of divine holiness cannot be avoided. But Christ has absorbed this fire: "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us" (2 Cor 5:21), taking the full heat of the divine judgment.
24.6: The bloody city with its rust: The rusty pot — the accumulated bloodguilt and moral contamination. The piece-by-piece removal without lot-casting: no preferences, all equally condemned. Christologically, the impartiality of the judgment (no lot-casting to spare any piece) corresponds to Paul's "all have sinned" (Rom 3:23) — there is no untainted piece in the covenant pot. This universal condemnation is the necessary precondition for the universal offer of grace.
24.7: Blood poured on bare rock, not covered with earth. The open, unatoned blood of injustice. Christologically, Abel's blood cried from the ground (Gen 4:10) — pointing to a blood that cries louder still. Hebrews 12:24 says Jesus's blood "speaks a better word than the blood of Abel." The uncovered blood of Jerusalem's injustice is addressed by the covered blood of Christ's sacrifice: "the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin" (1 John 1:7).
24.8: Blood put on bare rock to provoke wrath — the open accusation. The blood as the permanent visible accusation before YHWH. Christologically, Christ's blood serves the opposite function: it silences the accusation rather than amplifying it. "The accuser of our brothers" is overcome "by the blood of the Lamb" (Rev 12:10-11). Where the open blood of Jerusalem's injustice cries for vengeance, the blood of Christ cries "forgiveness."
24.9: YHWH himself makes the pile great — the divine amplification of the judgment. The intensification of the fire: not just natural consequence but active divine judgment. Christologically, the divine initiative in judgment corresponds to the divine initiative in salvation: "he chose us in him before the foundation of the world" (Eph 1:4). The same divine "I myself" (va'ani) who intensifies the judgment is the one who said "I myself will seek my lost sheep" (Ezek 34:11; Luke 15:4).
24.10: Heap on the wood, kindle the fire, cook the meat thoroughly — the complete consumption of the judgment. The thoroughness of the cooking mirrors the completeness required for atonement. Christologically, the completeness of Christ's atoning work: "by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified" (Heb 10:14). The thorough cooking of judgment corresponds to the thorough work of the cross.
24.11: The empty pot placed on the coals — the purification of the vessel after its contents are consumed. Even the structure requires purification after judgment. Christologically, the new creation is not merely the refilling of the old pot but its complete purification: "the first things have passed away... behold, I am making all things new" (Rev 21:4-5). The empty pot on the coals is the purified structure ready for new contents.
24.12: The rust that does not come out with fire — the unremovable contamination. The limit of purification-by-judgment: even intense fire cannot clean what only grace can address. Christologically, the inadequacy of judgment-alone purification is precisely the point of Hebrews 10:1-4: "it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins." The persistent rust requires what fire alone cannot provide — the priestly intercession of Christ who "always lives to make intercession" (Heb 7:25).
24.13: The filth of lewdness — purification attempted but not achieved. YHWH tried to purify through partial judgments but the contamination remained. Christologically, this is the diagnosis that makes the incarnation necessary: human moral failure cannot be remedied by escalating external discipline. The new covenant writes the law on the heart (Jer 31:33) — internal transformation rather than external correction — accomplished by the Spirit poured out through Christ's redemptive work.
24.14: The irreversible word: "I will not hold back, I will not spare, I will not relent." The triple non-relenting of divine judgment. Christologically, this absolute divine commitment to judgment is what makes substitutionary atonement necessary. God cannot simply overlook sin without injustice — the triple negation of v.14 is the righteousness that the cross satisfies. Romans 3:25-26: God presented Christ as a propitiation "to show his righteousness" — so that he might be just and the justifier of those who trust Jesus.
24.15: A word comes to Ezekiel concerning his wife. The transition from the pot-allegory to the most personal oracle in the entire book. Christologically, the move from cosmic judgment (the city) to intimate loss (the wife) mirrors the incarnation's move from cosmic lordship to personal sacrifice: "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us" (John 1:14). The divine commitment is not merely juridical-abstract but personally costly.
24.16: The desire of your eyes taken with a plague: YHWH will take Ezekiel's wife suddenly and Ezekiel must not mourn. The most costly prophetic sign-act in scripture — the prophet's own beloved as the instrument of divine communication. Christologically, this is the most direct preparation for "he did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all" (Rom 8:32). The Father who commanded Ezekiel not to mourn publicly is the Father who gave the Son he loved supremely. Both are acts of love made costly by love's own depth.
24.17: The prohibition on mourning — no sighing, no weeping, no mourning-bread. The counter-cultural command to withhold all outward grief. Christologically, the voluntary self-restriction of love — the Father who did not intervene to save the Son, the Son who prayed "not my will but yours be done" (Luke 22:42) — is foreshadowed in Ezekiel's obedient non-mourning. The discipline of love that can contain its grief for a greater purpose is the model of sacrificial love.
24.18: Ezekiel speaks to the people in the morning; his wife dies in the evening; in the morning he does not mourn. The three-beat sequence: prophecy → death → continued ministry without mourning. The Christological parallel is the resurrection pattern: crucifixion → death → resurrection and continued ministry. The prophet who continues to function after the loss that should have broken him is the sign of One who continues to intercede after the death that seemed to end everything.
24.19: The people ask: "Will you not tell us what these things mean for us?" — the standard prophetic sign-act question. The people's inquiry is always the sign-act's purpose: to prompt the question that allows the divine word to be received. Christologically, Jesus's actions consistently prompt this same question: "What is this? A new teaching with authority!" (Mark 1:27). The signs create the space for explanation; the explanation points to the greater reality.
24.20: Ezekiel reports that the word of YHWH came to him. The prophetic self-attribution — not his own imagination but divine communication. Christologically, Christ's words have the same self-attribution but at a higher level: "the word that you hear is not mine but the Father's who sent me" (John 14:24). The prophetic "thus says YHWH" becomes the Son's "my Father says" — same divine authority, different degree of intimacy.
24.21: The sanctuary as the people's "desire of their eyes": YHWH will profane his sanctuary, the pride and strength of the people, the desire of their eyes and the longing of their souls. Ezekiel's wife = the temple. The destruction of the most cherished covenantal institution mirrors the death of the most cherished personal relationship. Christologically, Jesus is "greater than the temple" (Matt 12:6) and his body is the true temple (John 2:19-22). The profaned physical temple is replaced by the living temple of his body and the community gathered in him (1 Cor 3:16; Eph 2:19-22).
24.22: The people will do as Ezekiel did: not cover the lips, not eat mourning-bread. The sign-act applied to the whole community. The community of non-mourning is not a community of no-feeling but of overwhelmed incapacity. Christologically, the church lives in a similar between-time: the sanctuary (Christ's body) appears to have been removed, yet the disciples do not mourn in the world's way. Paul's "we do not grieve as others do who have no hope" (1 Thess 4:13) is the resurrection-form of this non-mourning.
24.23: Turbans on heads, sandals on feet — the inverted signs (not removed as in mourning but maintained). The normal world-order continuing in the midst of incomprehensible loss. Christologically, the disciples at Pentecost continue normal life activities (they were "devoting themselves to prayer" while waiting, Acts 1:14) in the period between the ascension and the Spirit's coming. The between-time requires sustained presence and prayer rather than paralytic mourning.
24.24: Ezekiel as the sign: "Ezekiel shall be a sign to you; according to all that he has done you shall do." The prophet whose life is the message — not just the medium but the content of the communication. Christologically, Jesus is the ultimate sign: "the sign of the Son of Man" (Matt 24:30); "greater than Jonah" (Matt 12:41). But where Jonah and Ezekiel were signs pointing beyond themselves, Christ is the sign that is the reality — the signifier who is also the signified.
24.25: When YHWH takes the stronghold, the glory, the desire of their eyes and the longing of their souls — sons and daughters also — the fugitive comes. The comprehensive loss that precedes the opening of the mouth. Christologically, the comprehensive loss of the crucifixion — the disciples' despair on Good Friday — is the necessary precondition for the Easter joy. "Unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit" (John 12:24).
24.26: The fugitive who comes to open the ears — the news-bearer whose arrival unlocks the prophetic voice. The fugitive is the messenger of the completed judgment who liberates Ezekiel from his constrained speech. Christologically, the resurrection is the event that liberates the disciples from their post-crucifixion silence: "the disciples were glad when they saw the Lord" (John 20:20); Peter who denied three times became the one who preached to three thousand at Pentecost. The fugitive's news transforms silence into proclamation.
24.27: The opening of the mouth: On the day the fugitive arrives, Ezekiel's mouth is opened and he is silent no more. The end of the constrained speech that has characterized the judgment period. Christologically, this is the resurrection-unlocking of testimony. The disciples who were hidden "for fear of the Jews" (John 20:19) have their mouth opened by the risen Christ: "Peace be with you. As the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you" (John 20:21). The great commission is the eschatological opening of the prophetic mouth that Ezekiel 24:27 anticipates.

25.1: The oracle-word of YHWH introducing the four-nations section. The oracles against surrounding nations (chs 25-32) are framed within YHWH's universal sovereignty. Christologically, this section grounds the universality of Christ's lordship: "all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me" (Matt 28:18) — not just authority over Israel but over Ammon, Moab, Edom, Philistia, Tyre, Sidon, and Egypt. The Great Commission that follows from "all authority" has its prophetic foundation in the nations-oracles of the prophets.
25.2: Face set toward the Ammonites — the divine addressing of Israel's eastern neighbor. The prophetic oracle directed specifically to Ammon. Christologically, the specification of nations is the background for the universal mission: "Go and make disciples of all nations (panta ta ethnē)" (Matt 28:19) — precisely the nations that the OT prophets addressed in judgment are the nations the NT mission addresses with the gospel. Ammon (modern Jordan) is included in the universal scope of the new-covenant mission.
25.3: Ammon said "Aha!" over YHWH's sanctuary when it was profaned, over Israel when it was made desolate, over Judah when it went into exile. Malicious joy at the covenant people's ruin. Christologically, the nations who gloated over Israel's destruction are the nations Paul describes in Romans 11:18: "do not boast over the branches." The warning against gentile arrogance at Israel's partial hardening assumes that the same spirit that animated Ammon could animate the churches Paul addresses.
25.4: Given to the people of the east as a possession, their encampments and dwelling places established in Ammon. The historical judgment on Ammon — taken over by the eastern desert peoples. Christologically, the judgment on those who rejected the covenant sign (the temple's desolation) is the background for the warning of Hebrews 10:29: "how much worse punishment will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God." Greater privilege (knowing the covenant sign) entails greater accountability.
25.5: Rabbah becomes a pasture for camels and Ammon a fold for flocks — the reversal from urban civilization to pastoral wasteland. The recognition formula: "you shall know that I am YHWH." Christologically, this forced recognition of YHWH through judgment anticipates the voluntary recognition through faith. Every unwilling acknowledgment of YHWH's sovereignty in the OT points toward the willing acknowledgment that Paul describes: "at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord" (Phil 2:10-11).
25.6: Ammon clapped hands and stamped feet and rejoiced with full contempt over Israel. The physical expression of malicious joy — body language of contempt. Christologically, the contempt of the nations for the covenant people is most intensely expressed in the passion narrative: "they mocked him, saying Hail, King of the Jews!" (Matt 27:29). The clapping and stamping of Ammon is the prototype of the soldiers' contemptuous mockery of the King who was simultaneously condemned and crowned.
25.7: YHWH stretches out his hand against Ammon, gives them as plunder, cuts them off from the peoples. The divine counter-action against those who rejoiced at the covenant people's ruin. Christologically, the nations who were cut off from the peoples anticipate Paul's argument: "if you were cut from what is by nature a wild olive tree, and grafted, contrary to nature, into a cultivated olive tree, how much more will these, the natural branches, be grafted back into their own olive tree" (Rom 11:24). Cutting off and grafting in are both within YHWH's sovereign purpose.
25.8: Moab says "the house of Judah is like all the other nations." Moab's theological claim — denying Israel's covenant distinctiveness. Christologically, the denial of covenantal election is addressed by Paul in Romans 9-11: yes, all nations have sinned equally (Rom 3:23), but the gospel is "to the Jew first and also to the Greek" (Rom 1:16) — not because of inherent superiority but because of covenant history that culminates in the Messiah who comes from Israel (Rom 9:5).
25.9: The flank of Moab exposed from its border cities. The geographic vulnerability of Moab under divine judgment. Christologically, the exposing of what seemed secure — the border fortifications of Moab's identity — is the pattern of the gospel's encounter with every human security structure: "every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God" (2 Cor 10:5) taken captive to Christ.
25.10: Given to the sons of the East as Ammon was — the same fate shared by both Transjordanian kingdoms. The equalization of judgment across related nations. Christologically, the equal treatment of Ammon and Moab prepares for Paul's equal treatment of Jew and Greek: the same Lord "is Lord of all, bestowing his riches on all who call on him" (Rom 10:12) — the same Lord who judges all equally is the same Lord who saves all equally who trust him.
25.11: Judgments executed on Moab — the recognition formula closes the Moab oracle. "They shall know that I am YHWH." The forced recognition that Moab denied (v.8: "like all the other nations") is now imposed through judgment. Christologically, the ultimate forced recognition is the judgment at the end of the age: "every knee shall bow" (Phil 2:10) — both those who denied God's lordship and those who affirmed it will acknowledge it. The difference is whether the acknowledgment comes through faith now or through judgment then.
25.12: Edom acted revengefully against the house of Judah and incurred grievous guilt by taking revenge. Edom's specific crime: active revenge rather than passive schadenfreude. Christologically, Edom (as Esau, the elder twin who lost the blessing to Jacob/Israel) represents the principle of human-natural claim against divine election. Paul uses Esau/Edom explicitly: "Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated" (Rom 9:13 citing Mal 1:2-3) — the election of grace that bypasses natural precedence is the same election that chose the younger Son of David from Bethlehem over all the greater powers of his age.
25.13: Judgment on Edom from Teman to Dedan, by the sword. The geographic totality of Edom's judgment. Christologically, the Edomite territory (Idumea) became the region from which Herod the Great came — the Edomite king who sought to kill the infant Messiah (Matt 2:16-18). The ancient enmity between Esau/Edom and Jacob/Israel reaches its climax in Herod's massacre of the innocents, and is finally resolved when the one who came to save Esau's children as well as Jacob's (cf. v.53's inclusion of Sodom) completes his redemptive work.
25.14: Judgment through Israel: YHWH's vengeance on Edom will come through the hand of his people Israel. Unique among the four oracles: Edom's judgment is mediated through Israel. Christologically, the ultimate reversal of Edom-over-Israel (Esau's birthright, the Edomite kings listed in Gen 36 before any Israelite king) comes through the Israelite Messiah. The Son of David from Jacob's line is the fulfillment of the promise that "the elder will serve the younger" (Gen 25:23; Rom 9:12).
25.15: Philistia acted with perpetual enmity (eivat olam) and took revenge with scorn. The "perpetual enmity" reaching back to the earliest Israel-Philistia conflicts echoes the primal enmity of Genesis 3:15: "I will put enmity (eivah) between you and the woman, and between your offspring and her offspring." Christologically, the perpetual enmity that the seed of the serpent maintains against the covenant people is resolved by the one seed of the woman who crushes the serpent's head (Rom 16:20: "the God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet").
25.16: YHWH will cut off the Cherethites and destroy the remnant of the seacoast — the complete elimination of Philistia's power. The Philistine pentapolis destroyed. Christologically, the overthrow of the perpetual-enmity nations through divine judgment is the negative image of the positive reconciliation that Christ effects: "he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility" (Eph 2:14). The same hostility that animated the Philistines' perpetual enmity is the hostility that Christ broke down on the cross.
25.17: Great vengeance with furious rebukes — and they shall know YHWH: The strongest judgment language of the four oracles closes chapter 25. The recognition formula caps all four nations' judgments. Christologically, the universal recognition of YHWH as the goal of all the nations-judgments points to the universal acknowledgment that Christ's universal lordship will produce: "at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father" (Phil 2:10-11). The nations who will not acknowledge YHWH willingly are brought to acknowledgment through judgment; those who acknowledge him through faith do so joyfully in advance of that final reckoning.

26.1: The date header establishes that this oracle against Tyre was delivered in the same period as the fall of Jerusalem (the eleventh year, though the month is missing in the Hebrew — possibly a scribal gap). The historical coincidence is theologically significant: just as YHWH judges his own covenant city, he also judges the city that gloated over its fall. In Christ, this double-judgment structure is resolved: the cross is both the judgment of Jerusalem's sin (Matt 23:37-38; Luke 19:41-44) and the defeat of the world's proud systems (John 12:31: “now the ruler of this world will be cast out”).
26.2: Tyre's sin is gloating over Jerusalem's fall: “Aha! The gate of the peoples is broken; it has swung open to me.” The commercial city sees Jerusalem's destruction as an opportunity — trade routes through the Levantine corridor would be redirected through Tyre. Christ's parable of the tenants (Matt 21:33-44) echoes this dynamic: those who exploit the owner's absence to seize the inheritance will face the owner's return. Tyre's calculation — Jerusalem's loss is Tyre's gain — mirrors every worldly scheme that profits from the suffering of others. The same logic underlies Edom's treachery (Ezek 25:12; Obad 11-14). Christ's judgment reverses this inversion.
26.3: “Many nations against you as the sea brings up its waves” — YHWH's judgment on Tyre comes in waves, not a single blow: Nebuchadnezzar's 13-year siege (585-573 BCE), then Alexander's causeway conquest (332 BCE), then subsequent decline through the Hellenistic and Roman periods. This cumulative judgment anticipates the eschatological pattern in Revelation: the judgments come in sequences (seals, trumpets, bowls), each wave deeper than the last. Christ is the one who “rides on the waves” (Matt 14:25-26) — he is the Lord of the seas whose waves of judgment no earthly power can withstand.
26.4: “I will also scrape the soil from her and make her a bare rock” — the stripping of Tyre to bare rock is the judgment of exposure: what lay under the beauty and commerce is simply stone. Alexander the Great literally scraped the mainland rubble of Old Tyre into the sea to build his causeway to the island fortress in 332 BCE, leaving the mainland site bare — one of the most precise fulfillments of prophetic detail in ancient history. Christologically, the cross is the ultimate “bare rock” moment: the world's pretensions stripped away, nothing left but the bare truth of divine judgment and grace. Christ himself is the “rock” (1 Cor 10:4; Matt 7:24-25) — but it is not bare stone for destruction; it is the foundation of life.
26.5: “She will become a place in the middle of the sea for the spreading of nets” — the prophetic image is of fishermen using the ruins of the great city as a platform for drying fishing nets. The fishing imagery recalls Jesus's call of the disciples: “Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men” (Matt 4:19). Where Tyre spread commercial wealth across the seas, the disciples would spread the gospel net. The contrast is instructive: Tyre's nets caught material goods; the gospel net catches human souls. The place where commercial empire falls becomes the place from which the kingdom of God spreads.
26.6: “Her daughters on the mainland will be slain by the sword, and they shall know that I am the LORD” — the “daughters” are the dependent mainland settlements. The knowledge formula that follows every judgment announces the epistemological outcome: YHWH's identity is disclosed through his acts of judgment. Christ is the ultimate disclosure of YHWH's identity — not through judgment but through the cross and resurrection. The “they will know that I am the LORD” formula finds its NT fulfillment in Phil 2:10-11: “at the name of Jesus every knee shall bow and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord” — universal acknowledgment through the revelation of Christ's lordship.
26.7: Nebuchadnezzar is described as “king of kings” (melek hamelachim) — the supreme title of ANE monarchy, used also for Artaxerxes (Ezra 7:12) and in the NT as Christ's title (Rev 17:14; 19:16: “King of kings and Lord of lords”). The use of “king of kings” for Nebuchadnezzar, the temporary human instrument of divine judgment, is itself an implicit pointing beyond him: the true King of kings is the one who employs Nebuchadnezzar as his sword. Christ's title “King of kings” in Revelation asserts that what Nebuchadnezzar was called by flattery, Christ is by nature.
26.8: The detailed siege terminology (siege works, mound, siege engines, shields) describes the literal mechanics of Babylonian military conquest. Christ's conquest is accomplished by entirely opposite means: not with battering rams against walls but with the “sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God” (Eph 6:17), not by force but by the power of resurrection life (2 Cor 10:4-5: “the weapons of our warfare are not carnal but mighty in God for pulling down strongholds”). The contrast between Nebuchadnezzar's military siege and Christ's gospel conquest is the contrast between the first and last kingdoms.
26.9: Battering rams against walls and towers broken down — the progressive dismantling of Tyre's defenses mirrors the progressive revelation that no human security system is permanent. Hebrews 12:26-27 applies the shaking of earthly structures explicitly to Christ's kingdom: “this phrase, 'Yet once more,' indicates the removing of things that are shaken ... in order that the things that cannot be shaken may remain.” The walls of Tyre are among the “shakeable” things; Christ's kingdom is the “unshakeable” one that remains after all earthly powers are leveled.
26.10: “Your walls will shake at the noise of horsemen, wheels, and chariots when he enters your gates” — the overwhelming military force that causes walls to tremble contrasts with Christ's entry into Jerusalem on a donkey (Matt 21:5: “your king is coming to you, humble and mounted on a donkey”, citing Zech 9:9). The King of kings entered the world's great city not with the noise of war-horses but in deliberate humility. Yet this humble entry led to the judgment that Zech 9:9-10 itself anticipated: the dismantling of war-horses and chariots and the proclamation of peace “to the nations.”
26.11: “His horses' hooves will trample all your streets; he will slaughter your people with the sword and your strong pillars will collapse to the ground” — the strong pillars (matsevot oz) were Tyre's commemorative stelae and victory monuments. In Revelation 3:12 Christ promises to make the overcomer “a pillar in the temple of my God, never to go out of it” — the pillars that earthly powers erect and YHWH tears down are replaced with human beings incorporated permanently into the divine temple. The judgment of Tyre's pillars clears the way for a different kind of architectural permanence.
26.12: “They will cast your stones, your timber, and your soil into the sea” — this verse is strikingly fulfilled by Alexander's 332 BCE causeway: he used the ruins of mainland Tyre (stones, timber, and soil) as construction material, dragging them into the sea to build the road to the island city. The historical precision of this prophecy (verified by ancient sources including Arrian's Anabasis) is one of the strongest arguments for the predictive character of biblical prophecy. Christ fulfills prophecy not in hidden, allegorized ways but in sometimes startlingly concrete historical events that vindicate the prophetic claim.
26.13: “I will put an end to the noise of your songs, and the sound of your harps will be heard no more” — the silencing of Tyre's music is the direct source for Revelation 18:22: “the sound of harpists and musicians, of flute players and trumpeters, will be heard in you no more.” In Revelation 18, the fallen “Babylon” is a composite of every proud commercial civilization including Tyre. Christ's final judgment silences the music of worldly self-celebration — but it is simultaneously replaced by the “new song” of Rev 5:9 and 14:3, the song of the redeemed before the Lamb. One music ends; another begins.
26.14: “You will be a place for the spreading of nets and will never be rebuilt” — modern Tyre (Sur in Lebanon) does exist as a city, which has led to debate about this verse. The most precise interpretation is that the ancient Tyre known for its commercial glory was permanently ended — the island fortress that Alexander conquered never recovered its previous stature. Christ's fulfillment of Ezekiel's judgment-oracles is not always through direct historical completion but through the principle they embody: no worldly system that opposes God's purposes will ultimately stand. The “never be rebuilt” points to the eschatological finality of Christ's judgment (Rev 18:21: “Babylon the great city will be thrown down with violence and will be found no more”).
26.15: “Will not the coastlands tremble at the sound of your fall?” — the international trembling at Tyre's fall is the “fear of the LORD” motif made global: when YHWH acts in judgment, the nations recognize his power. Christ's cross and resurrection had the same effect — “the earth shook and the rocks split ... the centurion ... said, 'Surely he was the Son of God!'” (Matt 27:51-54). The trembling of the nations at divine action is consistent from Tyre's fall to the crucifixion to the final judgment.
26.16: “All the princes of the sea will descend from their thrones, remove their robes, and strip off their embroidered garments; they will clothe themselves with trembling” — this mourning gesture of the sea-princes is directly cited in Revelation 18:9-10: “the kings of the earth who committed immorality and lived in luxury with her, they will weep and wail over her ... standing far off ... saying, 'Alas! Alas! You great city, you mighty city, Babylon!'” The voluntary demotion of princes (removing robes, sitting on the ground) reflects the shock of seeing that even the mightiest commercial empire can fall. Before Christ's judgment seat, all earthly thrones are equally temporary.
26.17: The lament: “How you are destroyed, you who were inhabited by seafarers — you renowned city, mighty on the sea!” — the mourning over Tyre anticipates the mourning over Babylon in Rev 18 but also the mourning over Jerusalem that Jesus himself initiated (Matt 23:37-38; Luke 19:41-44). Jesus wept over Jerusalem with the same grief that Ezekiel's sea-princes show over Tyre — but with the additional dimension that he was simultaneously pronouncing and bearing the judgment. The one who laments the fall of the great city is also the one who executes the judgment that causes the fall.
26.18: “Now the coastlands tremble on the day of your fall; the islands in the sea are dismayed at your end” — the ripple effect of Tyre's judgment across the maritime world. In Christ's first coming, the response to his death and resurrection created exactly this kind of international trembling: from Jerusalem to Judea to Samaria to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8), the impact of the cross spread outward like waves from the center. The “islands” (iyim) of the prophets are often the distant Gentile territories (Isa 41:1; 42:4; 49:1) — the nations at the ends of the earth who will ultimately be brought to the knowledge of YHWH.
26.19: “When I bring up the deep over you and the great waters cover you” — the primordial waters (tehom) covering Tyre evokes the flood narrative (Gen 7:11: “all the springs of the great deep burst forth”) and reverses the creation narrative (the waters were restrained at creation so dry land appeared; here they are released again in un-creation). Christ's death is described in similar de-creation language: darkness covered the land (Matt 27:45; Amos 8:9), the temple curtain tore (Matt 27:51), the earth shook. The judgment of Tyre's un-creation is a type of the new creation that comes through Christ's death and resurrection — the old order passing away to make way for the new.
26.20: “I will bring you down with those who descend to the pit, to the ancient peoples ... but I will set glory in the land of the living” — this verse contains an extraordinary juxtaposition: while Tyre descends to the pit with the dead, YHWH will “set glory in the land of the living.” The glory (tzvi) set in the land of the living while the proud go to the pit is the resurrection-reversal: Christ descends to the realm of the dead (1 Pet 3:19; Acts 2:27 citing Ps 16:10: “you will not abandon my soul to Sheol”) but is set in glory — the land of the living — through the resurrection. The contrast between Tyre's permanent descent and the glory established in the land of the living is ultimately the contrast between death and resurrection.
26.21: “You will be no more. Though you are sought, you will never be found again” — the permanent disappearance of Tyre from the stage of history finds its eschatological completion in Revelation 18:21: “Babylon the great city will be thrown down with violence and will be found no more forever.” In Christ, the “will never be found again” verdict applies to every power that opposes God: “the last enemy to be destroyed is death” (1 Cor 15:26); “and the devil who had deceived them was thrown into the lake of fire” (Rev 20:10). What YHWH declared about Tyre is the template for the final verdict on every force of pride and opposition.

27.1: The introduction to the lament over Tyre as a ship is a simple date-and-call formula — but the form matters. The lament (qinah) is the dirge-form, the song of the dead. Ezekiel sings the death-song over Tyre before it has happened — the prophetic perfect. Christ employs the same prophetic mourning in Luke 19:41-44, weeping over Jerusalem before its destruction and describing its fate in prophetic past tense. The prophet who sings the dirge over the living city is like Christ who announces the coming judgment while simultaneously grieving it.
27.2: “Raise a lamentation over Tyre” — the call to lamentation places the prophet in the role of mourner-on-behalf-of-the-doomed. Ezekiel does not celebrate Tyre's fall but grieves it. This is the prophetic pathos that Heschel identified as the distinctive mark of Israelite prophecy: the prophet shares YHWH's grief over the necessity of judgment. Christ embodies this pathos most fully: he is simultaneously the judge who pronounces the verdict and the one who weeps over those condemned (John 11:35; Luke 19:41-44). The God who judges is the God who mourns.
27.3: Tyre's self-assessment: “I am perfect in beauty” (ani kelilat yofi) — the same claim Ezekiel directs ironically against Jerusalem in 16:14 (“your beauty was perfect through my splendor that I had bestowed on you”). Tyre claims inherent beauty; Jerusalem's beauty was derived. Christ alone is inherently “perfect in beauty” — Ps 45:2: “you are the most handsome of the sons of men; grace is poured upon your lips.” The beauty that Tyre claimed for itself and Jerusalem received as a gift is actually Christ's own — the beautiful one from whom all creaturely beauty derives.
27.4: “Your builders have perfected your beauty” — Tyre's beauty was constructed, engineered, designed. The builders who perfected the ship's beauty are human craftsmen. Christ's beauty, by contrast, is uncreated: he is the “radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature” (Heb 1:3) — not constructed but eternally generated. The ship's beauty will eventually be wrecked (v.26); the beauty of the incarnate Son is permanent and the foundation of the new creation's glory (Rev 21:11).
27.5: The ship's planks from Senir (the Amorite name for Hermon, Deut 3:9) and the mast from Lebanon cedars — the finest available timber. The cedar of Lebanon was the premier building material for temples (1 Kings 5:6-10), including Solomon's temple. Christ is the true temple (John 2:19-21) — the place where YHWH dwells — and the cedars that Tyre used for commerce, Solomon used for worship, and Ezekiel uses as an image of Assyrian imperial pride (ch.31), are the raw material of that which points to the divine dwelling in human flesh.
27.6: Oaks of Bashan and Cyprus ivory for the deck — the best materials from the east and west. The Bashan oaks were famous for their strength (Isa 2:13; Zech 11:2); Bashan is also associated with the Rephaim/giants and is a biblical symbol of formidable earthly strength. In Christ's kingship, he who “descended to the lower parts of the earth” (Eph 4:9 — to the realm where the Bashan-strength had no power over the dead) is the true master of all materials and domains.
27.7: Egyptian linen for the sail, purple and blue from Elishah (possibly Cyprus or the Aegean) for the awning — the finest luxury textiles. The purple and blue were the colors of the tabernacle curtains (Exod 26:1) and the high-priestly garments (Exod 28:4-6). Christ was clothed in purple by the soldiers in mockery (John 19:2) — unknowingly robing the High Priest in the colors of the sanctuary. The purple that commercial Tyre used for beauty, the tabernacle used for holiness, and the soldiers used for mockery, becomes in Revelation 19:13-16 the robe of Christ the conquering King.
27.8: Sidon and Arvad as oarsmen — skilled Phoenician sailors directing the ship. The skilled navigation of Tyre's commercial enterprise draws on the whole Phoenician maritime tradition. Christ calls fishermen (Matt 4:18-22) — not navigators of commerce but workers in the ancient subsistence economy — to be “fishers of men.” The contrast between Tyre's commercial pilots and Christ's fishing-disciples is the contrast between the economy of acquisition and the economy of grace.
27.9: “All the ships of the sea with their sailors were in you to trade your merchandise” — the universal commerce flowing to Tyre. In Christ, the nations flow not to a commercial hub but to a person: John 12:32: “I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.” The attraction of the cross replaces the attraction of the market — and the “all” who are drawn to Christ exceeds the “all the ships” that traded at Tyre.
27.10: Persian, Lydian, and Libyan soldiers in Tyre's army — a multiethnic mercenary force. The military power that Tyre's wealth could buy was formidable. Christ's army in Revelation 19:14 is “the armies of heaven, wearing fine linen, white and clean” — not mercenaries bought with commercial wealth but the redeemed clothed in Christ's righteousness. The contrast between Tyre's bought soldiers and Christ's redeemed army is the difference between a commercial transaction and a covenant relationship.
27.11: Arvad soldiers on the walls, Gammadim in the towers — military perfection, shields covering every surface, beauty made perfect through armament. Christ's armor is described in Eph 6:10-17, but it is fundamentally different: not bronze shields on a commercial ship's walls but the armor of truth, righteousness, peace, faith, and salvation. The defense of Tyre was its military hardware; the defense of Christ's people is his character and work applied to them.
27.12: Tarshish trading silver, iron, tin, and lead — the metals of ancient commerce. Tarshish is associated with the far west (possibly Spain or Sardinia) and represents the farthest reaches of the known trading world. The metals of Tarshish are raw materials for industry and weapons. In Christ, the metals are transformed: the “silver” of redemption (1 Pet 1:18: “you were ransomed ... not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ”) exceeds all commercial metals. The ransoming of humanity cost more than Tyre's entire treasury.
27.13: “They traded human beings and vessels of bronze in your markets” — Meshech and Tubal in slave trade and bronze. The commodification of human beings in the ancient commercial system is explicitly named. Christ's redemption is the direct reversal of the slave trade: he purchased people “for God from every tribe and language and people and nation” (Rev 5:9) — but not to own them as slaves but to make them “a kingdom and priests to our God.” The commerce that traded humans as commodities is overthrown by the One who paid for humans to be liberated into royal priesthood.
27.14: Beth-togarmah with horses, war horses, and mules — the cavalry-trade from Armenia (Togarmah = Armenia in later tradition). The horses of war supplied by Togarmah represent military power purchasable through commerce. Christ enters Jerusalem on a humble donkey (Matt 21:5; Zech 9:9), deliberately contrasting his arrival with the war-horse economy. The King who comes to Tyre is bought with Togarmah's war horses; the King who comes to Zion comes in humility. Revelation 19:11 reverses this: Christ comes on a white horse — but the horse is not purchased through commerce; it is the mount of divine judgment.
27.15: Dedan and the coastlands with ivory and ebony — luxury goods from Arabia and Africa. The ivory and ebony represent the exotic beauty that wealth could procure from the ends of the earth. The Magi who came to the infant Jesus brought “gold and frankincense and myrrh” (Matt 2:11) — luxury goods from the east, echoing the tribute of the nations flowing to Zion that Isaiah envisioned (Isa 60:5-6). What flowed to Tyre as commercial profit flows to Christ as worship: the wealth of the nations becomes the offering of the nations.
27.16: Syria with emeralds, purple, embroidered cloth, fine linen, coral, and rubies — the luxury goods of the Levantine trade. These are the same materials used in the tabernacle and priestly vestments: purple, embroidered cloth, and fine linen are the vocabulary of the sanctuary. Everything Tyre used for commercial display, the tabernacle used for divine service. Christ is the fulfillment of the tabernacle-pattern (John 1:14: “the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us”) — the sanctuary-materials that commercial empire appropriated are restored to their proper function as the dwelling-place of divine glory.
27.17: “Judah and the land of Israel were your traders; they traded wheat from Minnith, cakes, honey, olive oil, and balm” — Israel among Tyre's trading partners, supplying agricultural products. The covenant people are embedded in the commercial system of the nations. This is the diagnosis that the prophets repeatedly make: Israel's sin is not only idolatry but the commercialization of covenant life. Christ's cleansing of the temple (John 2:16: “Stop making my Father's house a house of trade”) is the definitive purification of the covenant community from Tyre's commercial spirit.
27.18: Damascus with wine and wool — the agricultural luxury products of Syria. The extensive merchant catalog (vv.12-24) is the OT's most detailed picture of ancient international commerce, mapping the trading world from Spain to India. It is the direct source for Rev 18's Babylon-lament catalog (vv.12-13) which lists 28 categories of luxury goods. The point of both catalogs is the same: all the world's material wealth, assembled and organized by human commercial genius, will be swept away in a moment. Only what Christ builds — his church (Matt 16:18) — will stand.
27.19: Dan and Javan (Greece) from Uzal with wrought iron, cassia, and fragrant calamus — iron for tools and weapons, spices for luxury use. The cassia and calamus are ingredients in the holy anointing oil of the tabernacle (Exod 30:23-25). The spices that flowed through Tyre's markets for commercial purchase were also the sacred ingredients of covenant worship. Christ's anointing with costly nard (Mark 14:3-9) is the NT equivalent: “wherever the gospel is proclaimed in the whole world, what she has done will be told in memory of her.” The commercial spice-trade is transformed into an act of devotion.
27.20: Dedan with saddlecloths for riding — equipment for horses and camels, the transportation infrastructure of ancient trade. The saddlecloths represent the practical underside of luxury commerce: the logistics, the animals, the equipment. Christ entered Jerusalem on a borrowed donkey with cloaks spread on the road (Matt 21:7-8) — the simplest possible version of royal transportation. The king who owned all the saddlecloths of Dedan came to his city in the humility that the commercial mind cannot recognize as royal.
27.21: Arabia and Kedar with lambs, rams, and goats — the pastoral economy of the desert peoples. The lambs and rams connect to the sacrificial system: Kedar's flocks supplied both commerce and, potentially, temple sacrifices. Christ is “the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world” (John 1:29) — the One to whom all the lambs of commercial and sacrificial trade ultimately pointed. The lambs traded through Tyre's markets were commercial commodities; the Lamb of God is the one whose sacrifice ends all commercial and sacrificial transactions.
27.22: “The merchants of Sheba and Raamah traded with you; they traded in the finest of all spices, all kinds of precious stones, and gold” — Sheba (southern Arabia / Ethiopia) with spices, precious stones, and gold echoes the Queen of Sheba's tribute to Solomon (1 Kings 10:2, 10: “she gave the king 120 talents of gold, large quantities of spices, and precious stones”) and anticipates the Magi's gifts to the infant Jesus (Matt 2:11: “they presented gifts of gold and frankincense and myrrh”). The wealth of nations flowing to YHWH's anointed king is a recurring pattern: Tyre monetizes it for commerce; Jesus receives it as eschatological tribute.
27.23: Haran, Canneh, Eden, Asshur, Chilmad — trade partners from Mesopotamia and northern Syria. The geographic extent of Tyre's trading network spans from Spain (Tarshish, v.12) to India (possibly Dedan's eastern connections) and from north Africa to Mesopotamia. This global commerce is the pre-modern world's version of what Paul describes as the principalities and powers ruling through “the elementary principles of the world” (Gal 4:3; Col 2:8): the organized systems of human commerce that bind people to the material world. Christ's liberation from these systems (Col 2:15: “disarming the rulers and authorities ... triumphing over them in the cross”) is the defeat of the commercial empire behind every Tyre.
27.24: “Choice garments, in blue and embroidered cloth, in chests of fine clothing bound with cords” — the luxury clothing trade. Clothing in the OT carries theological weight: the garments of Adam and Eve (Gen 3:21, YHWH provides clothing after the fall), the high-priestly garments (Exod 28), the robe of righteousness (Isa 61:10), the filthy garments of Joshua the high priest (Zech 3:3-5). Christ's seamless tunic (John 19:23-24, soldiers cast lots for it citing Ps 22:18) is the NT clothing-symbol: the garment that cannot be divided, the covering that surpasses all Tyre's blue and embroidered robes.
27.25: “The ships of Tarshish carried your merchandise; you were filled and made very glorious in the heart of the seas” — Tyre at its peak: “filled and very glorious.” The glory of the commercial empire at its apex is the prelude to its destruction (v.26). The pattern of “fullness leading to fall” is the prophetic diagnosis of Tyre, Assyria, Babylon, and ultimately of every self-sufficient civilization. Christ's fullness (pleroma) is the opposite: “in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily” (Col 2:9) — not the fullness of accumulated commercial wealth but the fullness of divine being, which cannot be wrecked by any east wind.
27.26: “The east wind has wrecked you in the heart of the seas” — the east wind is YHWH's instrument of judgment throughout the OT: the east wind dried up the Red Sea (Exod 14:21), brought the plague of locusts (Exod 10:13), destroyed Jonah's shade (Jon 4:8), and scattered Israel (Ps 48:7). Tyre's merchant ship is wrecked by the same wind that YHWH wields in covenant judgment. Christ, who “rebuked the wind and the raging waves” (Luke 8:24), is the master of the east wind that wrecks Tyre — the same power that judges is the power that stills the storm for his people.
27.27: “All will sink into the heart of the seas on the day of your ruin” — the totality of the sinking is emphasized: wealth, merchandise, goods, mariners, pilots, caulkers, dealers, soldiers — everything. The all-encompassing loss echoes the rich man in Luke 12:20: “You fool! This night your soul is required of you, and the things you have prepared, whose will they be?” The day of ruin comes suddenly, and nothing of the commercial structure survives. Only what is built on Christ — “a stone of testing, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation” (Isa 28:16) — survives the wreck.
27.28: “At the sound of the cry of your pilots the countryside shakes” — the lament of the skilled navigators causes the land itself to tremble. The “shaking of the earth” motif in prophetic texts is regularly associated with divine judgment and theophany (Isa 2:21; 13:13; Hag 2:6-7; Heb 12:26-27). The cry of Tyre's pilots — the most skilled, the highest-paid, the most celebrated — signals the collapse of human expertise before the divine judgment. “I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and the discernment of the discerning I will thwart” (1 Cor 1:19, citing Isa 29:14) — Christ crucified is “foolishness to Greeks” (1 Cor 1:23), the cosmic rebuke of all human navigational skill.
27.29: “All who handle the oar — the mariners and all the pilots of the sea — will come down from their ships and stand on the shore” — the maritime community's abandonment of their vessels in mourning for Tyre. They stand on solid ground having left the sea — the instability of the commercial enterprise becomes apparent only in the moment of catastrophic loss. Christ invites his disciples to come down from their boats: Peter walks on the water (Matt 14:29-30), then sinks, then is caught by Christ. The invitation to leave the commercial sea and stand on solid ground is the gospel's call.
27.30: “They will raise their voices over you and cry out bitterly; they will throw dust on their heads and roll in ashes” — the mourning rituals of the ancient world: dust on the head, rolling in ashes. These are the gestures of the deepest grief (Job 2:12-13; Lam 3:29; Josh 7:6). The mourning that the nations perform over Tyre is a parody of genuine repentance: they grieve the loss of their commercial partner, not their sin. Christ's call is to genuine mourning over sin (Matt 5:4: “blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted”) — the sorrow that leads to repentance, not merely the grief of material loss.
27.31: Shaved heads and sackcloth for Tyre — the mourning rituals that in the prophets belong to genuine lament for sin (Joel 1:13; Isa 22:12). The merchants perform the form of mourning without the substance. Christ's beatitude (Matt 5:4) and Paul's distinction (2 Cor 7:10: “godly grief produces repentance leading to salvation, but worldly grief produces death”) draws exactly this line: the grief over Tyre's fall is worldly grief, producing nothing but despair. The grief over one's sin before Christ is godly grief, producing repentance and life.
27.32: “Who was ever destroyed like Tyre, like one silenced in the midst of the sea?” — the rhetorical question of Tyre's uniqueness. The same question was asked of Babylon in Jer 50:23 (“how the hammer of the whole earth has been cut down and broken!”) and Rev 18:18 (“What city was like the great city?”). The lament over unprecedented destruction is itself a theological claim: YHWH's judgment is uniquely comprehensive, not like ordinary military defeat. Christ's cross is also unprecedented: “Has it ever happened in all your days or in the days of your fathers? Tell your children about it, and let your children tell their children” (Joel 1:2-3) — the singular, unrepeatable event at the center of history.
27.33: “When your merchandise went out over the seas, you satisfied many peoples; with your great wealth and goods you enriched the kings of the earth” — Tyre's positive contribution to the ancient world is acknowledged: it genuinely satisfied and enriched. This is not merely hypocritical praise; Tyre's commercial network did create real prosperity for many. The judgment is not that commercial activity is evil but that Tyre claimed ultimate credit and status for what YHWH provided. Christ satisfies far more comprehensively than Tyre: “I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst” (John 6:35) — the satisfaction that Tyre's trade offered temporarily, Christ offers permanently.
27.34: “Now you are wrecked by the seas, in the depths of the waters; your merchandise and all your company have sunk with you” — the wrecking is complete. The “depths of the waters” (maamaqqim mayim) recalls Ps 69:2 (“I have come into deep waters, and the flood sweeps over me”), the lament of the righteous sufferer — whose words Christ quotes from the cross (Ps 69:21 in John 19:28-29). The depths that swallow Tyre are the same depths that Christ entered in his Passion — but from the depths Christ rose, and from those depths he raises his people (Ps 130:1: “Out of the depths I cry to you, O LORD”).
27.35: “All the inhabitants of the coastlands are appalled at you; their kings shudder with horror and their faces are convulsed with fear” — the face-convulsion of the kings at Tyre's fall echoes the suffering servant's effect: “so his appearance was marred beyond human semblance, and his form beyond that of the children of mankind — so shall he sprinkle many nations; kings shall shut their mouths because of him” (Isa 52:14-15). The horror that Tyre's fall produces in kings is matched by the awestruck silence that the servant's suffering and exaltation produces. Both the fall of commercial empire and the exaltation of the suffering servant silence kings.
27.36: “The merchants among the peoples hiss at you; you have come to a horrible end and will be no more forever” — the merchants' hissing (sharak, a gesture of contemptuous horror) at Tyre's fall is echoed in Lam 2:15 (“all who pass your way hiss and wag their heads at the daughter of Jerusalem”) and in the crowd at the crucifixion (Matt 27:39: “those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads”). The same contemptuous gesture that the merchants direct at fallen Tyre was directed at Christ on the cross. What looks like the hissing at ultimate failure is, in Christ, the moment of ultimate victory — the moment when the world's contempt meets its match in the Father's vindication through resurrection.

28.1: The word-of-the-LORD formula introduces the first Tyre oracle of this chapter, addressed to the prince (nasi) of Tyre. The distinction between prince (nasi, vv.1-10) and king (melek, vv.11-19) is significant: the prince-oracle addresses the historical ruler (probably Ethbaal III, who ruled during Nebuchadnezzar's siege); the king-oracle employs a more elevated register that exceeds the historical ruler. Christ is both the antitype of the pride condemned in vv.1-10 and the fulfillment of the perfection described and lost in vv.12-19.
28.2: “Because your heart is proud and you have said, 'I am a god'” — the primal sin of hubris: claiming divine status. This is the sin of Adam (Gen 3:5: “you will be like God”), of the builders of Babel (Gen 11:4), of Pharaoh (Exod 5:2: “Who is the LORD that I should obey him?”), and will be the sin of the man of lawlessness (2 Thess 2:4: “who opposes and exalts himself against every so-called god or object of worship, so that he takes his seat in the temple of God, proclaiming himself to be God”). Christ, who truly is God, “did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped” (Phil 2:6) — the exact reversal of the Tyre-prince's sin: the human who claimed to be God vs. the God who became human.
28.3: “Behold, you are wiser than Daniel — no secret is hidden from you” — the ironic comparison to Daniel, the living standard of wisdom in Ezekiel's time (also in Ezek 14:14, 20). The prince of Tyre claims a wisdom that surpasses even the wisest of humans. Paul's response to this claim, applied to Christ: “in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Col 2:3). What the prince of Tyre claimed falsely — wisdom that surpasses Daniel, all secrets known — Christ possesses truly. The one who knew the secrets of every person's heart (John 2:24-25: “he knew all people ... he himself knew what was in man”) is the true answer to the wisdom-claim.
28.4: “By your wisdom and understanding you have gained wealth” — Tyre's wisdom produced commercial prosperity, paralleling Solomon's wisdom that produced material wealth (1 Kings 3:12-13: “I give you a wise and discerning heart ... I also give you what you have not asked, both riches and honor”). But Tyre's wisdom is self-attributing: it is credited to the prince's own intelligence, not to YHWH's gift. Proverbs 8 personifies Wisdom as the one through whom kings reign and wealth is found (Prov 8:15-21); Christ is “the wisdom of God” (1 Cor 1:24) in whom this figure is embodied. True wisdom produces true wealth (Prov 8:18) — Christ's riches are “unsearchable” (Eph 3:8).
28.5: “Your heart has become proud because of your wealth” — the sequence wisdom → wealth → pride is the diagnostic pattern. Deut 8:17-18 warns Israel against exactly this: “beware lest you say in your heart, 'My power and the might of my hand have gotten me this wealth.' You shall remember the LORD your God, for it is he who gives you power to get wealth.” Christ addresses the same pride in the parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) and warns: “a person's life does not consist in the abundance of his possessions” (Luke 12:15). The wealth that becomes pride is the seed of the judgment that follows in vv.6-10.
28.6: “Because you have set your heart as the heart of a god” — the therefore-of-judgment follows the because-of-sin. The structural logic of covenant lawsuit: accusation (vv.2-5) → verdict (v.6) → sentence (vv.7-10). Christ absorbs this lawsuit structure in his own passion: he is accused (John 19:7), sentenced (John 19:15-16), and executed — but the sentence he receives is not for his own pride but for ours. The covenant-lawsuit that the proud deserve falls on the innocent one, reversing the expected outcome.
28.7: “I will bring strangers against you, the most ruthless of the nations; they will draw their swords against the beauty of your wisdom and defile your splendor” — Babylon against Tyre's wisdom. What looks like military invasion is the divine deconstruction of false wisdom. Paul uses similar language for the cross: “God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong” (1 Cor 1:27). The “ruthless nations” that YHWH brings against proud wisdom are a type of the divine folly of the cross that defeats human wisdom more effectively than any army.
28.8: “They will thrust you down into the Pit, and you will die the death of the slain in the heart of the seas” — the Pit (shachat) is Sheol, the place of the dead. The prince who claimed to sit in the seat of gods is thrust into the lowest place. This is the precise reversal of the Magnificat pattern: “he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate” (Luke 1:52). Christ himself enters the Pit — “you will not abandon my soul to Hades” (Acts 2:27, Ps 16:10) — but rises from it, unlike the prince of Tyre whose descent is permanent.
28.9: “Will you still say, 'I am a god,' before the one who kills you? But you will be a mere man in the hand of the one who wounds you” — the death-chamber revelation: divinity claims dissolve before the sword. This is the prophetic demythologization of human pretension: in the moment of death, all claims to divine status are exposed as lies. Christ's death on the cross appeared to be the same demythologization of his divine claims (Mark 15:29-32: “let him come down from the cross and we will believe him”). But the resurrection is YHWH's counter-verdict: what looked like the exposure of Christ's messianic claim as false was the vindication of that claim in the most definitive possible way.
28.10: “You will die the death of the uncircumcised by the hand of foreigners” — the maximum shame of the Ezekielian Sheol-gallery (see ch.32). The prince who claimed divine status will die in the company of the uncircumcised — those without covenant identity. This is the judgment that falls on all who claim divine status without possessing it. Christ, who is truly divine, willingly accepted the shameful death of a criminal (crucifixion was the death of slaves and rebels, not of citizens and the covenanted) — absorbing the shame that should fall on those who falsely claim divine status, so that those who genuinely belong to him are covered by his righteousness.
28.11: The second word-of-the-LORD formula introduces the king-oracle (vv.11-19) with a higher register than the prince-oracle. The shift from “prince” to “king” signals a different level of discourse: the king-oracle will exceed what can be said of any merely human ruler. The dual oracle structure (prince → king) mirrors other prophetic dual-level oracles (Isa 14:4-21 speaks both of the king of Babylon and, many interpreters argue, of a transcendent figure behind him; Dan 10:13, 20-21 speaks of the spiritual “prince” of Persia and Greece behind the earthly kings).
28.12: “You were the seal of perfection, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty” — these attributes cannot apply to any historical king of Tyre: no human ruler has ever been “the seal of perfection” (totem tachlit). The figure described is one who began in a state of genuine, complete perfection — wisdom without limit, beauty without flaw. The only being to whom this can be said truthfully is Christ: “the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature” (Heb 1:3); “the image of the invisible God” (Col 1:15). What the king of Tyre's ode describes as a state now lost, Christ embodies as a permanent identity.
28.13: “You were in Eden, the garden of God” — the placement of this figure in Eden, the primordial divine garden, exceeds any historical king. The gemstones listed (ruby, topaz, crystal, beryl, onyx, jasper, lapis lazuli, turquoise, emerald) are nearly identical to those on the high priest's breastplate (Exod 28:17-20), suggesting this figure was adorned as a priestly guardian of the divine presence. “The craftsmanship of your settings and engravings was prepared in you on the day you were created” — a created being, but created for the highest possible role. Many interpreters (from Origen to modern scholars) read this as describing the being behind the king of Tyre: a guardian cherub who had access to Eden but fell. Christologically, Christ is the one who restores Eden-access (Rev 2:7: “to the one who conquers I will grant to eat of the tree of life, which is in the paradise of God”).
28.14: “You were the anointed guardian cherub; I had appointed you. You were on the holy mountain of God; you walked among the stones of fire” — the cherub (kerub) was the specific category of heavenly being that guarded sacred space: the cherubim on the ark (Exod 25:18-22), the cherubim in the Holy of Holies (1 Kings 6:23-28), the cherubim that drove Adam and Eve from Eden (Gen 3:24). “Anointed” (mimshach) — the same root as Messiah (mashiach). The anointed guardian cherub was set apart for the highest function: protecting the divine presence. Christ is the anointed one (Ps 2:2; Acts 4:26-27) who also guards and gives access to the divine presence — not by excluding sinners from the throne but by opening the way for them through his blood (Heb 10:19-22).
28.15: “You were blameless in your ways from the day you were created until iniquity was found in you” — the blamelessness that characterized this being from creation is the perfection Christ alone permanently retains. Jesus was “without sin” (Heb 4:15), “holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners” (Heb 7:26), “a lamb without blemish or spot” (1 Pet 1:19). The “until iniquity was found” applies to the figure in the oracle, not to Christ — but the contrast is precisely the Christological point: where the guardian cherub fell from blamelessness, Christ maintained it through the full course of his life, qualifying him to be the sufficient sacrifice.
28.16: “Through the abundance of your trade you were filled inwardly with violence, and you sinned; so I cast you out as profane from the mountain of God” — the fall: inward violence, sin, expulsion from the holy mountain. Luke 10:18: “I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven” — Jesus's reference to a primordial expulsion from the divine mountain is the NT's closest parallel to this verse. The casting out of the guardian cherub from the mountain of God is what Christ came to undo: he entered the territory of the fallen guardian (the world, which John describes as lying in the power of the evil one, 1 John 5:19) and defeated him (John 12:31; Rev 20:10).
28.17: “Your heart was proud because of your beauty; you corrupted your wisdom for the sake of your splendor. I have cast you to the ground and exposed you before kings” — the pride of beauty corrupting wisdom is the original pattern of spiritual fall: the created glory becoming the occasion for self-exaltation rather than YHWH-glorification. Christ is the one who “came to serve and not to be served” (Mark 10:45) and whose beauty was hidden in humiliation (Isa 53:2: “he had no form or majesty that we should look at him, and no beauty that we should desire him”). The one whose eternal beauty was uncorrupted by pride was exposed before kings — not in shame, but in the definitive act of divine love.
28.18: “By the abundance of your iniquities ... you defiled your sanctuaries; therefore I brought out fire from your midst — it consumed you, and I turned you to ashes on the earth” — fire from within consuming the sinful figure. This is the self-consuming nature of sin: the fire of judgment begins from the inside, not the outside. Christ absorbed this fire at Golgotha — “our God is a consuming fire” (Heb 12:29) and Christ bore the divine fire on behalf of sinners, so that those who are united to him are not consumed. The ashes on the earth where Tyre's glory stood become the ashes of repentance from which resurrection rises.
28.19: “All who knew you among the peoples are appalled at you; you have become a horror and shall be no more forever” — the permanent end of the one who fell from perfection. This permanent end is the theological statement about the irreversibility of the fall from the guardian cherub's original estate — unlike human sinners for whom restoration is possible (and for whom Christ came), the figure behind the king of Tyre oracle has passed the point of no return. Revelation 20:10 confirms this: the devil “will be tormented day and night forever and ever.” Christ's redemption is for the human beings who were deceived by the fallen cherub, not for the cherub itself — this is the scope and limit of the atonement's application.
28.20: The third word-of-the-LORD formula introduces the brief Sidon oracle (vv.21-23). Sidon, Tyre's northern neighbor, is addressed in a shorter oracle without the extended lament of the Tyre oracles. The structure of chs.25-28 systematically covers the nations surrounding Israel: Ammon, Moab, Edom, Philistia (ch.25), Tyre (chs.26-28), and Sidon (28:20-23). Christ's gospel eventually reaches all these nations (Rom 15:16-19: “from Jerusalem and all the way around to Illyricum I have fulfilled the ministry of the gospel of Christ”; Acts 27:3 mentions Sidon as one of Paul's ports of call).
28.21: “Son of man, set your face against Sidon and prophesy against it” — the confrontation formula applied to Sidon. Sidon was the northern Phoenician city-state, older than Tyre and historically important in the patriarchal period (Gen 10:15: Sidon is Canaan's firstborn son). Jesus himself traveled to the region of Tyre and Sidon and healed a Canaanite/Syrophoenician woman (Matt 15:21-28; Mark 7:24-30) — the judgment-oracle over Sidon becomes the location of a healing that Jesus uses to expand his mission beyond Israel. The judgments of the OT become the opportunities of the NT.
28.22: “I will be glorified in your midst ... I will display my holiness in her” — YHWH's holiness is displayed through judgment: “I am holy in those who are near me, and before all the people I will be glorified” (Lev 10:3, after Nadab and Abihu's death). Christ is “the Holy One of God” (Mark 1:24; John 6:69) whose holiness is displayed not through punitive destruction but through the cross, where divine holiness and divine love meet in perfect unity. Sidon witnesses YHWH's glory; the whole world witnesses Christ's glory in the cross and resurrection (John 12:23-24: “the hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified ... unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies”).
28.23: “The slain will fall in her midst by the sword that comes against her from every side — and they will know that I am the LORD” — the knowledge formula after judgment. The historical fulfillment came through Babylonian, Persian, and Seleucid conquests of Sidon (Sidon was razed by Artaxerxes III in 345 BCE). The “they will know” formula finds its deepest fulfillment in the crucifixion narrative: the Roman centurion who witnessed the manner of Christ's death said “truly this man was the Son of God” (Mark 15:39) — the knowledge of YHWH's identity coming through the death of the one who bears his name.
28.24: “There will no longer be a painful brier or a piercing thorn from any of the surrounding peoples who showed contempt for Israel” — the removal of briers and thorns. The thorn-brier imagery goes back to the curse of Genesis 3:18: “thorns and thistles it shall bring forth for you.” The surrounding nations are described as thorns in Israel's side (Num 33:55; Josh 23:13). Christ wore a crown of thorns at the crucifixion (Matt 27:29; John 19:2) — visually absorbing the curse of the thorns, taking onto himself the “brier” that the nations represent, so that “there will no longer be a painful brier.” The removal of the thorn-nations in Ezekiel's oracle is accomplished Christologically through Christ's absorption of the curse.
28.25: “When I gather the house of Israel from the peoples among whom they have been scattered, and display my holiness in them before the eyes of the nations, then they will dwell in their own land that I gave to my servant Jacob” — the restoration promise after the judgment oracles. The gathering of scattered Israel is the eschatological gathering that the NT applies to Christ: “he died ... to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad” (John 11:52). The Jacob-land promise is fulfilled in the new creation where “the meek shall inherit the earth” (Matt 5:5; cf. Ps 37:11) — the land given to Jacob's offspring becomes, in Christ, the renewed earth given to all who are Abraham's children by faith.
28.26: “They will dwell in it safely and build houses and plant vineyards. They will live with confidence when I have executed judgments against all their surrounding neighbors who showed them contempt. And they will know that I am the LORD their God” — the concluding restoration promise. Safety, houses, vineyards, confidence — the covenant blessing formula from Lev 26:5 (“you shall live in your land securely”) and Mic 4:4 (“every man shall sit under his vine and under his fig tree”).
